paper_a.title: Efficient On-Device Inference with Quantized Large Language Models
paper_a.summary: Recent advances in 4-bit quantization (GGUF/llama.cpp) allow 7B-13B parameter LLMs to run on consumer CPUs and mobile devices with minimal accuracy loss. Offline inference is now feasible on a standard laptop without GPU hardware.
paper_a.key_insight: LLMs can run fully offline on a teacher's laptop using 4-bit quantization with no cloud dependency.

paper_b.title: Teacher Time Allocation and Burnout: A Multi-Country Study
paper_b.summary: Teachers spend 50-60% of their working hours on administrative tasks including grading, attendance tracking, and lesson planning rather than direct student instruction. This administrative burden correlates strongly with burnout rates and reduced teaching quality.
paper_b.key_insight: Teachers spend more than half their time on administrative work, leaving less than half for actual teaching.

patent_c.title: Adaptive Knowledge State Estimation System Using Bayesian Inference
patent_c.summary: A patented system that tracks student knowledge state across skill nodes using Bayesian inference over sequences of question-response pairs. The system identifies knowledge gaps and recommends the next optimal question to maximise learning efficiency.
patent_c.key_insight: Bayesian knowledge tracing can identify exactly which concept a student doesn't understand and prescribe the next question.

startup_d.title: QuizAI — Cloud-Based AI Assessment Platform
startup_d.summary: A venture-backed startup building cloud-based AI quiz and formative assessment tools for K-12 classrooms. The platform requires persistent internet connectivity and transmits student response data to external servers, raising FERPA compliance concerns for US schools.
startup_d.key_insight: Existing AI assessment tools are cloud-only and create student data privacy risks that block adoption in privacy-conscious districts.

packs.human_panel_baseline: An offline, privacy-preserving Socratic tutoring system that runs entirely on the teacher's local device using a quantized LLM. It uses knowledge tracing to identify individual student gaps in real-time during class, auto-generates adaptive follow-up questions, and produces grading summaries automatically — freeing at least 30% of teacher administrative time without requiring cloud connectivity or sharing student data with any external server. This is non-obvious because it requires synthesising four independent insights: (1) LLMs now run offline, (2) teachers are drowning in admin work, (3) knowledge tracing can prescribe the next question, and (4) privacy concerns are blocking cloud-based tools.
